In general, if the return target is not in the input list, the caller is expected to invoke\\n37: \\t// Scheme.New(target) and then perform a conversion between the current Go type and the destination Go type.\\n38: \\t// Sophisticated implementations may use additional information about the input kinds to pick a destination kind.\\n39: \\tKindForGroupVersionKinds(kinds []schema.GroupVersionKind) (target schema.GroupVersionKind, ok bool)\\n40: \\t// Identifier returns string representation of the object.\\n41: \\t// Identifiers of two different encoders should be equal only if for every input\\n42: \\t// kinds they return the same result.\\n43: \\tIdentifier() string\\n44: }\\n45: \\n46: // Identifier represents an identifier.\\n47: // Identitier of two different objects should be equal if and only if for every\\n48: // input the output they produce is exactly the same.\\n49: type Identifier string\\n50: \\n51: // Encoder writes objects to a serialized form\\n52: type Encoder interface {\\n53: \\t// Encode writes an object to a stream. Implementations may return errors if the versions are\\n54: \\t// incompatible, or if no conversion is defined.\\n55: \\tEncode(obj Object, w io.Writer) error\\n56: \\t// Identifier returns an identifier of the encoder.\\n57: \\t// Identifiers of two different encoders should be equal if and only if for every input\\n58: \\t// object it will be encoded to the same representation by both of them.\\n59: \\t//\\n60: \\t// Identifier is intended for use with CacheableObject#CacheEncode method. In order to\\n61: \\t// correctly handle CacheableObject, Encode() method should look similar to below, where\\n62: \\t// doEncode() is the encoding logic of implemented encoder:\\n63: \\t//   func (e *MyEncoder) Encode(obj Object, w io.Writer) error {\\n64: \\t//     if co, ok := obj.(CacheableObject); ok {\\n65: \\t//       return co.CacheEncode(e.Identifier(), e.doEncode, w)\\n66: \\t//     }\\n67: \\t//     return e.doEncode(obj, w)\\n68: \\t//   }\\n69: \\tIdentifier() Identifier\\n70: }\\n71: \\n72: // NondeterministicEncoder is implemented by Encoders that can serialize objects more efficiently in\\n73: // cases where the output does not need to be deterministic.\\n74: type NondeterministicEncoder interface {\\n75: \\tEncoder\\n76: \\n77: \\t// EncodeNondeterministic writes an object to the stream. Unlike the Encode method of\\n78: \\t// Encoder, EncodeNondeterministic does not guarantee that any two invocations will write\\n79: \\t// the same sequence of bytes to the io.Writer. Any differences will not be significant to a\\n80: \\t// generic decoder. For example, map entries and struct fields might be encoded in any\\n81: \\t// order.\\n82: \\tEncodeNondeterministic(Object, io.Writer) error\\n83: }\\n84: \\n85: // MemoryAllocator is responsible for allocating memory.\\n86: // By encapsulating memory allocation into its own interface, we can reuse the memory\\n87: // across many operations in places we know it can significantly improve the performance.\\n88: type MemoryAllocator interface {\\n89: \\t// Allocate reserves memory for n bytes.\\n90: \\t// Note that implementations of this method are not required to zero the returned array.\\n91: \\t// It is the caller's responsibility to clean the memory if needed.\\n92: \\tAllocate(n uint64) []byte\\n93: }\\n94: \\n95: // EncoderWithAllocator  serializes objects in a way that allows callers to manage any additional memory allocations.\\n96: type EncoderWithAllocator interface {\\n97: \\tEncoder\\n98: \\t// EncodeWithAllocator writes an object to a stream as Encode does.\\n99: \\t// In addition, it allows for providing a memory allocator for efficient memory usage during object serialization\\n100: \\tEncodeWithAllocator(obj Object, w io.Writer, memAlloc MemoryAllocator) error\\n101: }\\n102: \\n103: // Decoder attempts to load an object from data.\\n104: type Decoder interface {\\n105: \\t// Decode attempts to deserialize the provided data using either the innate typing of the scheme or the\\n106: \\t// default kind, group, and version provided. It returns a decoded object as well as the kind, group, and\\n107: \\t// version from the serialized data, or an error. If into is non-nil, it will be used as the target type\\n108: \\t// and implementations may choose to use it rather than reallocating an object. However, the object is not\\n109: \\t// guaranteed to be populated. The returned object is not guaranteed to match into. If defaults are\\n110: \\t// provided, they are applied to the data by default. If no defaults or partial defaults are provided, the\\n111: \\t// type of the into may be used to guide conversion decisions.\\n112: \\tDecode(data []byte, defaults *schema.GroupVersionKind, into Object) (Object, *schema.GroupVersionKind, error)\\n113: }\\n114: \\n115: // Serializer is the core interface for transforming objects into a serialized format and back.\\n116: // Implementations may choose to perform conversion of the object, but no assumptions should be made.\\n117: type Serializer interface {\\n118: \\tEncoder\\n119: \\tDecoder\\n120: }\\n121: \\n122: // Codec is a Serializer that deals with the details of versioning objects. It offers the same\\n123: // interface as Serializer, so this is a marker to consumers that care about the version of the objects\\n124: // they receive.\\n125: type Codec Serializer\\n126: \\n127: // ParameterCodec defines methods for serializing and deserializing API objects to url.Values and\\n128: // performing any necessary conversion. Unlike the normal Codec, query parameters are not self describing\\n129: // and the desired version must be specified.\\n130: type ParameterCodec interface {\\n131: \\t// DecodeParameters takes the given url.Values in the specified group version and decodes them\\n132: \\t// into the provided object, or returns an error.\\n133: \\tDecodeParameters(parameters url.Values, from schema.GroupVersion, into Object) error\\n134: \\t// EncodeParameters encodes the provided object as query parameters or returns an error.\\n135: \\tEncodeParameters(obj Object, to schema.GroupVersion) (url.Values, error)\\n136: }\\n137: \\n138: // Framer is a factory for creating readers and writers that obey a particular framing pattern.\\n139: type Framer interface {\\n140: \\tNewFrameReader(r io.ReadCloser) io.ReadCloser\\n141: \\tNewFrameWriter(w io.Writer) io.Writer\\n142: }\\n143: \\n144: // SerializerInfo contains information about a specific serialization format\\n145: type SerializerInfo struct {\\n146: \\t// MediaType is the value that represents this serializer over the wire.\\n147: \\tMediaType string\\n148: \\t// MediaTypeType is the first part of the MediaType (\\\"application\\\" in \\\"application/json\\\").\\n149: \\tMediaTypeType string\\n150: \\t// MediaTypeSubType is the second part of the MediaType (\\\"json\\\" in \\\"application/json\\\").\\n151: \\tMediaTypeSubType string\\n152: \\t// EncodesAsText indicates this serializer can be encoded to UTF-8 safely.\\n153: \\tEncodesAsText bool\\n154: \\t// Serializer is the individual object serializer for this media type.\\n155: \\tSerializer Serializer\\n156: \\t// PrettySerializer, if set, can serialize this object in a form biased towards\\n157: \\t// readability.\\n158: \\tPrettySerializer Serializer\\n159: \\t// StrictSerializer, if set, deserializes this object strictly,\\n160: \\t// erring on unknown fields.\\n161: \\tStrictSerializer Serializer\\n162: \\t// StreamSerializer, if set, describes the streaming serialization format\\n163: \\t// for this media type.\\n164: \\tStreamSerializer *StreamSerializerInfo\\n165: }\\n166: \\n167: // StreamSerializerInfo contains information about a specific stream serialization format\\n168: type StreamSerializerInfo struct {\\n169: \\t// EncodesAsText indicates this serializer can be encoded to UTF-8 safely.\\n170: \\tEncodesAsText bool\\n171: \\t// Serializer is the top level object serializer for this type when streaming\\n172: \\tSerializer\\n173: \\t// Framer is the factory for retrieving streams that separate objects on the wire\\n174: \\tFramer\\n175: }\\n176: \\n177: // NegotiatedSerializer is an interface used for obtaining encoders, decoders, and serializers\\n178: // for multiple supported media types. This would commonly be accepted by a server component\\n179: // that performs HTTP content negotiation to accept multiple formats.\\n180: type NegotiatedSerializer interface {\\n181: \\t// SupportedMediaTypes is the media types supported for reading and writing single objects.\\n182: \\tSupportedMediaTypes() []SerializerInfo\\n183: \\n184: \\t// EncoderForVersion returns an encoder that ensures objects being written to the provided\\n185: \\t// serializer are in the provided group version.\\n186: \\tEncoderForVersion(serializer Encoder, gv GroupVersioner) Encoder\\n187: \\t// DecoderToVersion returns a decoder that ensures objects being read by the provided\\n188: \\t// serializer are in the provided group version by default.\\n189: \\tDecoderToVersion(serializer Decoder, gv GroupVersioner) Decoder\\n190: }\\n191: \\n192: // ClientNegotiator handles turning an HTTP content type into the appropriate encoder.\\n193: // Use NewClientNegotiator or NewVersionedClientNegotiator to create this interface from\\n194: // a NegotiatedSerializer.\\n195: type ClientNegotiator interface {\\n196: \\t// Encoder returns the appropriate encoder for the provided contentType (e.g. application/json)\\n197: \\t// and any optional mediaType parameters (e.g. pretty=1), or an error. If no serializer is found\\n198: \\t// a NegotiateError will be returned. The current client implementations consider params to be\\n199: \\t// optional modifiers to the contentType and will ignore unrecognized parameters.\\n200: \\tEncoder(contentType string, params map[string]string) (Encoder, error)\\n201: \\t// Decoder returns the appropriate decoder for the provided contentType (e.g. application/json)\\n202: \\t// and any optional mediaType parameters (e.g. pretty=1), or an error. If no serializer is found\\n203: \\t// a NegotiateError will be returned. The current client implementations consider params to be\\n204: \\t// optional modifiers to the contentType and will ignore unrecognized parameters.\\n205: \\tDecoder(contentType string, params map[string]string) (Decoder, error)\\n206: \\t// StreamDecoder returns the appropriate stream decoder for the provided contentType (e.g.\\n207: \\t// application/json) and any optional mediaType parameters (e.g. pretty=1), or an error. If no\\n208: \\t// serializer is found a NegotiateError will be returned. The Serializer and Framer will always\\n209: \\t// be returned if a Decoder is returned. The current client implementations consider params to be\\n210: \\t// optional modifiers to the contentType and will ignore unrecognized parameters.\\n211: \\tStreamDecoder(contentType string, params map[string]string) (Decoder, Serializer, Framer, error)\\n212: }\\n213: \\n214: // StorageSerializer is an interface used for obtaining encoders, decoders, and serializers\\n215: // that can read and write data at rest. This would commonly be used by client tools that must\\n216: // read files, or server side storage interfaces that persist restful objects.\\n217: type StorageSerializer interface {\\n218: \\t// gv GroupVersioner) Decoder\\n231: }\\n232: \\n233: // NestedObjectEncoder is an optional interface that objects may implement to be given\\n234: // an opportunity to encode any nested Objects / RawExtensions during serialization.\\n235: type NestedObjectEncoder interface {\\n236: \\tEncodeNestedObjects(e Encoder) error\\n237: }\\n238: \\n239: // NestedObjectDecoder is an optional interface that objects may implement to be given\\n240: // an opportunity to decode any nested Objects / RawExtensions during serialization.\\n241: // It is possible for DecodeNestedObjects to return a non-nil error but for the decoding\\n242: // to have succeeded in the case of strict decoding errors (e.g. unknown/duplicate fields).\\n243: // As such it is important for callers of DecodeNestedObjects to check to confirm whether\\n244: // an error is a runtime.StrictDecodingError before short circuiting.\\n245: // Similarly, implementations of DecodeNestedObjects should ensure that a runtime.StrictDecodingError\\n246: // is only returned when the rest of decoding has succeeded.\\n247: type NestedObjectDecoder interface {\\n248: \\tDecodeNestedObjects(d Decoder) error\\n249: }\\n250: \\n251: ///////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////\\n252: // Non-codec interfaces\\n253: \\n254: type ObjectDefaulter interface {\\n255: \\t// Default \\tConvertToVersion(in Object, gv GroupVersioner) (out Object, err error)\\n262: \\tPrioritizedVersionsForGroup(group string) []schema.GroupVersion\\n263: }\\n264: \\n265: // ObjectConvertor converts an object to a different version.\\n266: type ObjectConvertor interface {\\n267: \\t// Convert attempts to convert one object into another, or returns an error. This\\n268: \\t// method does not mutate the in object, but the in and out object might share data structures,\\n269: \\t// i.e. the out object cannot be mutated without mutating the in object as well.\\n270: \\t// The context argument will be passed to all nested conversions.\\n271: \\tConvert(in, out, context interface{}) error\\n272: \\t// ConvertToVersion takes the provided object and converts it the provided version. The <encode> function will\\n346: \\t// be used in case of cache miss. The <encode> function takes ownership\\n347: \\t// of the object.\\n348: \\t// If CacheableObject is a wrapper, then deep-copy of the wrapped object\\n349: \\t// should be passed to <encode> function.\\n350: \\t// CacheEncode assumes that for two different calls with the same <id>,\\n351: \\t// <encode> function will also be the same.\\n352: \\tCacheEncode(id Identifier, encode func(Object, io.Writer) error, w io.Writer) error\\n353: \\t// GetObject returns a deep-copy of an object to be encoded - the caller of\\n354: \\t// GetObject() is the owner of returned object. The reason for making a copy\\n355: \\t// is to avoid bugs, where caller modifies the object and forgets to copy it,\\n356: \\t// thus modifying the object for everyone.\\n357: \\t// The object returned by GetObject should be the same as the one that is supposed\\n358: \\t// to be passed to <encode> function in CacheEncode method.\\n359: \\t// If CacheableObject is a wrapper, the copy of wrapped object should be returned.\\n360: \\tGetObject() Object\\n361: }\\n362: \\n363: // Unstructured objects store values as map[string]interface{}, with only values that can be serialized\\n364: // to JSON allowed.\\n365: type Unstructured interface {\\n366: \\tObject\\n367: \\t// NewEmptyInstance returns a new instance of the concrete type containing only kind/apiVersion and no other data.\\n368: \\t// This should be called instead of reflect.New() for unstructured types because the go type alone does not preserve kind/apiVersion info.\\n369: \\tNewEmptyInstance() Unstructured\\n370: \\t// UnstructuredContent returns a non-nil map with this object's contents. \\t\\\"k8s.io/apimachinery/pkg/runtime/schema\\\"\\n24: )\\n25: \\n26: const (\\n27: \\t// APIVersionInternal may be used if you are registering a type that should not\\n28: \\t// be considered stable of possible conversion targets into a single option.\\n34: type GroupVersioner interface {\\n35: \\t// KindForGroupVersionKinds returns a desired target group version kind for the given input, or returns ok false if no\\n36: \\t// target is known. In general, if the return target is not in the input list, the caller is expected to invoke\\n37: \\t// Scheme.New(target) and then perform a conversion between the current Go type and the destination Go type.\\n38: \\t// Sophisticated implementations may use additional information about the input kinds to pick a destination kind.\\n39: \\tKindForGroupVersionKinds(kinds []schema.GroupVersionKind) (target schema.GroupVersionKind, ok bool)\\n40: \\t// Identifier returns string representation of the object.\\n41: \\t// Identifiers of two different encoders should be equal only if for every input\\n42: \\t// kinds they return the same result.\\n43: \\tIdentifier() string\\n44: }\\n45: \\n46: // Identifier represents an identifier.\\n47: // Identitier of two different objects should be equal if and only if for every\\n48: // input the output they produce is exactly the same.\\n49: type Identifier string\\n50: \\n51: // Encoder writes objects to a serialized form\\n52: type Encoder interface {\\n53: \\t// Encode writes an object to a stream. Implementations may return errors if the versions are\\n54: \\t// incompatible, or if no conversion is defined.\\n55: \\tEncode(obj Object, w io.Writer) error\\n56: \\t// Identifier returns an identifier of the encoder.\\n57: \\t// Identifiers of two different encoders should be equal if and only if for every input\\n58: \\t// object it will be encoded to the same representation by both of them.\\n59: \\t//\\n60: \\t// Identifier is intended for use with CacheableObject#CacheEncode method. In order to\\n61: \\t// correctly handle CacheableObject, Encode() method should look similar to below, where\\n62: \\t// doEncode() is the encoding logic of implemented encoder:\\n63: \\t//   func (e *MyEncoder) Encode(obj Object, w io.Writer) error {\\n64: \\t//     if co, ok := obj.(CacheableObject); ok {\\n65: \\t//       return co.CacheEncode(e.Identifier(), e.doEncode, w)\\n66: \\t//     }\\n67: \\t//     return e.doEncode(obj, w)\\n68: \\t//   }\\n69: \\tIdentifier() Identifier\\n70: }\\n71: \\n72: // NondeterministicEncoder is implemented by Encoders that can serialize objects more efficiently in\\n73: // cases where the output does not need to be deterministic.\\n74: type NondeterministicEncoder interface {\\n75: \\tEncoder\\n76: \\n77: \\t// EncodeNondeterministic writes an object to the stream. Unlike the Encode method of\\n78: \\t// Encoder, EncodeNondeterministic does not guarantee that any two invocations will write\\n79: \\t// the same sequence of bytes to the io.Writer. Any differences will not be significant to a\\n80: \\t// generic decoder. For example, map entries and struct fields might be encoded in any\\n81: \\t// order.\\n82: \\tEncodeNondeterministic(Object, io.Writer) error\\n83: }\\n84: \\n85: // MemoryAllocator is responsible for allocating memory.\\n86: // By encapsulating memory allocation into its own interface, we can reuse the memory\\n87: // across many operations in places we know it can significantly improve the performance.\\n88: type MemoryAllocator interface {\\n89: \\t// Allocate reserves memory for n bytes.\\n90: \\t// Note that implementations of this method are not required to zero the returned array.\\n91: \\t// It is the caller's responsibility to clean the memory if needed.\\n92: \\tAllocate(n uint64) []byte\\n93: }\\n94: \\n95: // EncoderWithAllocator  serializes objects in a way that allows callers to manage any additional memory allocations.\\n96: type EncoderWithAllocator interface {\\n97: \\tEncoder\\n98: \\t// EncodeWithAllocator writes an object to a stream as Encode does.\\n99: \\t// In addition, it allows for providing a memory allocator for efficient memory usage during object serialization\\n100: \\tEncodeWithAllocator(obj Object, w io.Writer, memAlloc MemoryAllocator) error\\n101: }\\n102: \\n103: // Decoder attempts to load an object from data.\\n104: type Decoder interface {\\n105: \\t// Decode attempts to deserialize the provided data using either the innate typing of the scheme or the\\n106: \\t// default kind, group, and version provided. It returns a decoded object as well as the kind, group, and\\n107: \\t// version from the serialized data, or an error. If into is non-nil, it will be used as the target type\\n108: \\t// and implementations may choose to use it rather than reallocating an object. However, the object is not\\n109: \\t// guaranteed to be populated. The returned object is not guaranteed to match into. In general, if the return target is not in the input list, the caller is expected to invoke\n37: \t// Scheme.New(target) and then perform a conversion between the current Go type and the destination Go type.\n38: \t// Sophisticated implementations may use additional information about the input kinds to pick a destination kind.\n39: \tKindForGroupVersionKinds(kinds []schema.GroupVersionKind) (target schema.GroupVersionKind, ok bool)\n40: \t// Identifier returns string representation of the object.\n41: \t// Identifiers of two different encoders should be equal only if for every input\n42: \t// kinds they return the same result.\n43: \tIdentifier() string\n44: }\n45: \n46: // Identifier represents an identifier.\n47: // Identitier of two different objects should be equal if and only if for every\n48: // input the output they produce is exactly the same.\n49: type Identifier string\n50: \n51: // Encoder writes objects to a serialized form\n52: type Encoder interface {\n53: \t// Encode writes an object to a stream. Implementations may return errors if the versions are\n54: \t// incompatible, or if no conversion is defined.\n55: \tEncode(obj Object, w io.Writer) error\n56: \t// Identifier returns an identifier of the encoder.\n57: \t// Identifiers of two different encoders should be equal if and only if for every input\n58: \t// object it will be encoded to the same representation by both of them.\n59: \t//\n60: \t// Identifier is intended for use with CacheableObject#CacheEncode method. In order to\n61: \t// correctly handle CacheableObject, Encode() method should look similar to below, where\n62: \t// doEncode() is the encoding logic of implemented encoder:\n63: \t//   func (e *MyEncoder) Encode(obj Object, w io.Writer) error {\n64: \t//     if co, ok := obj.(CacheableObject); ok {\n65: \t//       return co.CacheEncode(e.Identifier(), e.doEncode, w)\n66: \t//     }\n67: \t//     return e.doEncode(obj, w)\n68: \t//   }\n69: \tIdentifier() Identifier\n70: }\n71: \n72: // NondeterministicEncoder is implemented by Encoders that can serialize objects more efficiently in\n73: // cases where the output does not need to be deterministic.\n74: type NondeterministicEncoder interface {\n75: \tEncoder\n76: \n77: \t// EncodeNondeterministic writes an object to the stream. Unlike the Encode method of\n78: \t// Encoder, EncodeNondeterministic does not guarantee that any two invocations will write\n79: \t// the same sequence of bytes to the io.Writer. Any differences will not be significant to a\n80: \t// generic decoder. A resource may have only one of {ObjectMeta, ListMeta}.\\n61: type ListMeta struct {\\n62: \\t// Deprecated: selfLink is a legacy read-only field that is no longer populated by the system.\\n63: \\t// +optional\\n64: \\tSelfLink string `json:\\\"selfLink,omitempty\\\" protobuf:\\\"bytes,1,opt,name=selfLink\\\"`\\n65: \\n66: \\t// String that identifies the server's internal version of this object that\\n67: \\t// can be used by clients to determine when objects have changed.\\n68: \\t// Value must be treated as opaque by clients and passed unmodified back to the server.\\n69: \\t// Populated by the system.\\n70: \\t// Read-only.\\n71: \\t// More info: https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/devel/sig-architecture/api-conventions.md#concurrency-control-and-consistency\\n72: \\t// +optional\\n73: \\tResourceVersion string `json:\\\"resourceVersion,omitempty\\\" protobuf:\\\"bytes,2,opt,name=resourceVersion\\\"`\\n74: \\n75: \\t// continue may be set if the user set a limit on the number of items returned, and indicates that\\n76: \\t// the server has more data available. The value is opaque and may be used to issue another request\\n77: \\t// to the endpoint that served this list to retrieve the next set of available objects. Continuing a\\n78: \\t// consistent list may not be possible if the server configuration has changed or more than a few\\n79: \\t// minutes have passed. The resourceVersion field returned when using this continue value will be\\n80: \\t// identical to the value in the first response, unless you have received this token from an error\\n81: \\t// message.\\n82: \\tContinue string `json:\\\"continue,omitempty\\\" protobuf:\\\"bytes,3,opt,name=continue\\\"`\\n83: \\n84: \\t// remainingItemCount is the number of subsequent items in the list which are not included in this\\n85: \\t// list response. If the list request contained label or field selectors, then the number of\\n86: \\t// remaining items is unknown and the field will be left unset and omitted during serialization.\\n87: \\t// If the list is complete (either because it is not chunking or because this is the last chunk),\\n88: \\t// then there are no more remaining items and this field will be left unset and omitted during\\n89: \\t// serialization.\\n90: \\t// Servers older than v1.15 do not set this field.\\n91: \\t// The intended use of the remainingItemCount is *estimating* the size of a collection. Clients\\n92: \\t// should not rely on the remainingItemCount to be set or to be exact.\\n93: \\t// +optional\\n94: \\tRemainingItemCount *int64 `json:\\\"remainingItemCount,omitempty\\\" protobuf:\\\"bytes,4,opt,name=remainingItemCount\\\"`\\n95: }\\n96: \\n97: // Field path constants that are specific to the internal API\\n98: // representation.\\n99: const (\\n100: \\tObjectNameField = \\\"metadata.name\\\"\\n101: )\\n102: \\n103: // These are internal finalizer values for Kubernetes-like APIs, must be qualified name unless defined here\\n104: const (\\n105: \\tFinalizerOrphanDependents = \\\"orphan\\\"\\n106: \\tFinalizerDeleteDependents = \\\"foregroundDeletion\\\"\\n107: )\\n108: \\n109: // ObjectMeta is metadata that all persisted resources must have, which includes all objects\\n110: // users must create.\\n111: type ObjectMeta struct {\\n112: \\t// Name must be unique within a namespace. Name is primarily intended for creation idempotence and configuration\\n115: \\t// definition.\\n116: \\t// Cannot be updated.\\n117: \\t// More info: https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/overview/working-with-objects/names#names\\n118: \\t// +optional\\n119: \\tName string `json:\\\"name,omitempty\\\" protobuf:\\\"bytes,1,opt,name=name\\\"`\\n120: \\n121: \\t// GenerateName is an optional prefix, used by the server, to generate a unique\\n122: \\t// name ONLY IF the Name field has not been provided.\\n123: \\t// If this field is used, the name returned to the client will be different\\n124: \\t// than the name passed. This value will also be combined with a unique suffix.\\n125: \\t// The provided value has the same validation rules as the Name field,\\n126: \\t// and may be truncated by the length of the suffix required to make the value\\n127: \\t// unique on the server.\\n128: \\t//\\n129: \\t// If this field is specified and the generated name exists, the server will return a 409.\\n130: \\t//\\n131: \\t// Applied only if Name is not specified.\\n132: \\t// More info: https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/devel/sig-architecture/api-conventions.md#idempotency\\n133: \\t// +optional\\n134: \\tGenerateName string `json:\\\"generateName,omitempty\\\" protobuf:\\\"bytes,2,opt,name=generateName\\\"`\\n135: \\n136: \\t// Namespace defines the space within which each name must be unique. An empty namespace is\\n137: \\t// equivalent to the \\\"default\\\" namespace, but \\\"default\\\" is the canonical representation.\\n138: \\t// Not all objects are required to be scoped to a namespace - external (serialized) types that lack their own version (e.g TypeMeta)\n21: //   - internal (never-serialized) types that are needed by several different\n22: //     api groups, and so live here, to avoid duplication and/or import loops\n23: //     (e.g. LabelSelector).\n24: //\n25: // In the future, we will probably move these categories of objects into\n26: // separate packages.\n27: package v1\n28: \n29: import (\n30: \t\"fmt\"\n31: \t\"strings\"\n32: \n33: \t\"k8s.io/apimachinery/pkg/runtime\"\n34: \t\"k8s.io/apimachinery/pkg/types\"\n35: )\n36: \n37: // TypeMeta describes an individual object in an API response or request\n38: // with strings representing the type of the object and its API schema version.\n39: // Structures that are versioned or persisted should inline TypeMeta.\n40: //\n41: // +k8s:deepcopy-gen=false\n42: type TypeMeta struct {\n43: \t// Kind is a string value representing the REST resource this object represents.\n44: \t// Servers may infer this from the endpoint the client submits requests to.\n45: \t// Cannot be updated.\n46: \t// In CamelCase.\n47: \t// More info: https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/devel/sig-architecture/api-conventions.md#types-kinds\n48: \t// +optional\n49: \tKind string `json:\"kind,omitempty\" protobuf:\"bytes,1,opt,name=kind\"`\n50: \n51: \t// APIVersion defines the versioned schema of this representation of an object.\n52: \t// Servers should convert recognized schemas to the latest internal value, and\n53: \t// may reject unrecognized values.\n54: \t// More info: https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/devel/sig-architecture/api-conventions.md#resources\n55: \t// +optional\n56: \tAPIVersion string `json:\"apiVersion,omitempty\" protobuf:\"bytes,2,opt,name=apiVersion\"`\n57: }\n58: \n59: // ListMeta describes metadata that synthetic resources must have, including lists and\n60: // various status objects. A resource may have only one of {ObjectMeta, ListMeta}.\n61: type ListMeta struct {\n62: \t// Deprecated: selfLink is a legacy read-only field that is no longer populated by the system.\n63: \t// +optional\n64: \tSelfLink string `json:\"selfLink,omitempty\" protobuf:\"bytes,1,opt,name=selfLink\"`\n65: \n66: \t// String that identifies the server's internal version of this object that\n67: \t// can be used by clients to determine when objects have changed.\n68: \t// Value must be treated as opaque by clients and passed unmodified back to the server.\n69: \t// Populated by the system.\n70: \t// Read-only.\n71: \t// More info: https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/devel/sig-architecture/api-conventions.md#concurrency-control-and-consistency\n72: \t// +optional\n73: \tResourceVersion string `json:\"resourceVersion,omitempty\" protobuf:\"bytes,2,opt,name=resourceVersion\"`\n74: \n75: \t// continue may be set if the user set a limit on the number of items returned, and indicates that\n76: \t// the server has more data available. The value is opaque and may be used to issue another request\n77: \t// to the endpoint that served this list to retrieve the next set of available objects. Continuing a\n78: \t// consistent list may not be possible if the server configuration has changed or more than a few\n79: \t// minutes have passed. The resourceVersion field returned when using this continue value will be\n80: \t// identical to the value in the first response, unless you have received this token from an error\n81: \t// message.\n82: \tContinue string `json:\"continue,omitempty\" protobuf:\"bytes,3,opt,name=continue\"`\n83: \n84: \t// remainingItemCount is the number of subsequent items in the list which are not included in this\n85: \t// list response. If the list request contained label or field selectors, then the number of\n86: \t// remaining items is unknown and the field will be left unset and omitted during serialization.\n87: \t// If the list is complete (either because it is not chunking or because this is the last chunk),\n88: \t// then there are no more remaining items and this field will be left unset and omitted during\n89: \t// serialization.\n90: \t// Servers older than v1.15 do not set this field.\n91: \t// The intended use of the remainingItemCount is *estimating* the size of a collection. Clients\n92: \t// should not rely on the remainingItemCount to be set or to be exact.\n93: \t// +optional\n94: \tRemainingItemCount *int64 `json:\"remainingItemCount,omitempty\" protobuf:\"bytes,4,opt,name=remainingItemCount\"`\n95: }\n96: \n97: // Field path constants that are specific to the internal API\n98: // representation.\n99: const (\n100: \tObjectNameField = \"metadata.name\"\n101: )\n102: \n103: // These are internal finalizer values for Kubernetes-like APIs, must be qualified name unless defined here\n104: const (\n105: \tFinalizerOrphanDependents = \"orphan\"\n106: \tFinalizerDeleteDependents = \"foregroundDeletion\"\n107: )\n108: \n109: // ObjectMeta is metadata that all persisted resources must have, which includes all objects\n110: // users must create.\n111: type ObjectMeta struct {\n112: \t// Name must be unique within a namespace. it will be created before the pod starts,\\n180: \\t// and deleted when the pod is removed.\\n181: \\t//\\n182: \\t// Use this if:\\n183: \\t// a) the volume is only needed while the pod runs,\\n184: \\t// b) features of normal volumes like restoring from snapshot or capacity\\n185: \\t//    tracking are needed,\\n186: \\t// c) the storage driver is specified through a storage class, and\\n187: \\t// d) the storage driver supports dynamic volume provisioning through\\n188: \\t//    a PersistentVolumeClaim (see EphemeralVolumeSource for more\\n189: \\t//    information on the connection between this volume type\\n190: \\t//    and PersistentVolumeClaim).\\n191: \\t//\\n192: \\t// Use PersistentVolumeClaim or one of the vendor-specific\\n193: \\t// APIs for volumes that persist for longer than the lifecycle\\n194: \\t// of an individual pod.\\n195: \\t//\\n196: \\t// Use CSI for light-weight local ephemeral volumes if the CSI driver is meant to\\n197: \\t// be used that way - see the documentation of the driver for\\n198: \\t// more information.\\n199: \\t//\\n200: \\t// A pod can use both types of ephemeral volumes and\\n201: \\t// persistent volumes at the same time.\\n202: \\t//\\n203: \\t// +optional\\n204: \\tEphemeral *EphemeralVolumeSource `json:\\\"ephemeral,omitempty\\\" protobuf:\\\"bytes,29,opt,name=ephemeral\\\"`\\n205: \\t// image represents an OCI object (a container image or artifact) pulled and mounted on the kubelet's host machine.\\n206: \\t// The volume is resolved at pod startup depending on which PullPolicy value is provided:\\n207: \\t//\\n208: \\t// - Always: the kubelet always attempts to pull the reference. Container creation will fail If the pull fails.\\n209: \\t// - Never: the kubelet never pulls the reference and only uses a local image or artifact. Container creation will fail if the reference isn't present.\\n210: \\t// - IfNotPresent: the kubelet pulls if the reference isn't already present on disk. Container creation will fail if the reference isn't present and the pull fails.\\n211: \\t//\\n212: \\t// The volume gets re-resolved if the pod gets deleted and recreated, which means that new remote content will become available on pod recreation.\\n213: \\t// A failure to resolve or pull the image during pod startup will block containers from starting and may add significant latency. Failures will be retried using normal volume backoff and will be reported on the pod reason and message.\\n214: \\t// The types of objects that may be mounted by this volume are defined by the container runtime implementation on a host machine and at minimum must include all valid types supported by the container image field.\\n215: \\t// The OCI object gets mounted in a single directory (spec.containers[*].volumeMounts.mountPath) by merging the manifest layers in the same way as for container images.\\n216: \\t// The volume will be mounted read-only (ro) and non-executable files (noexec).\\n217: \\t// Sub path mounts for containers are not supported (spec.containers[*].volumeMounts.subpath) before 1.33.\\n218: \\t// The field spec.securityContext.fsGroupChangePolicy has no effect on this volume type.\\n219: \\t// +featureGate=ImageVolume\\n220: \\t// +optional\\n221: \\tImage *ImageVolumeSource `json:\\\"image,omitempty\\\" protobuf:\\\"bytes,30,opt,name=image\\\"`\\n222: }\\n223: \\n224: // PersistentVolumeClaimVolumeSource references the user's PVC in the same namespace.\\n225: // This volume finds the bound PV and mounts that volume for the pod. \\\"SIGRTMAX\\\"\\n3212: )\\n3213: \\n3214: // Lifecycle describes actions that the management system should take in response to container lifecycle\\n3215: // events. For the PostStart and PreStop lifecycle handlers, management of the container blocks\\n3216: // until the action is complete, unless the container process fails, in which case the handler is aborted.\\n3217: type Lifecycle struct {\\n3218: \\t// PostStart is called immediately after a container is created. If the handler fails,\\n3219: \\t// the container is terminated and restarted according to its restart policy.\\n3220: \\t// Other management of the container blocks until the hook completes.\\n3221: \\t// More info: https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/containers/container-lifecycle-hooks/#container-hooks\\n3222: \\t// +optional\\n3223: \\tPostStart *LifecycleHandler `json:\\\"postStart,omitempty\\\" protobuf:\\\"bytes,1,opt,name=postStart\\\"`\\n3224: \\t// PreStop is called immediately before a container is terminated due to an\\n3225: \\t// API request or management event such as liveness/startup probe failure,\\n3226: \\t// preemption, resource contention, etc. The handler is not called if the\\n3227: \\t// container crashes or exits. The Pod's termination grace period countdown begins before the\\n3228: \\t// PreStop hook is executed. Regardless of the outcome of the handler, the\\n3229: \\t// container will eventually terminate within the Pod's termination grace\\n3230: \\t// period (unless delayed by finalizers).       NodeSelectorOperator = \\\"Lt\\\"\\n3808: )\\n3809: \\n3810: // A topology selector term represents the result of label queries.\\n3811: // A null or empty topology selector term matches no }\\n3931: \\n3932: // Defines a set of pods (namely those matching the labelSelector\\n3933: // relative to the given namespace(s)) that this pod should be\\n3934: // co-located (affinity) or not protobuf:\\\"bytes,4,opt,name=namespaceSelector\\\"`\\n3963: \\t// MatchLabelKeys is a set of pod label keys to select which pods will\\n3964: \\t// be taken into consideration. The keys are used to lookup values from the\\n3965: \\t// incoming pod labels, those key-value labels are merged with `labelSelector` as `key in (value)`\\n3966: \\t// to select the group of existing pods which pods will be taken into consideration\\n3967: \\t// for the incoming pod's pod (anti) affinity. Keys that don't exist in the incoming\\n3968: \\t// pod labels will be ignored. ephemeral containers.\\n5183: \\t// +optional\\n5184: \\tLifecycle *Lifecycle `json:\\\"lifecycle,omitempty\\\" protobuf:\\\"bytes,12,opt,name=lifecycle\\\"`\\n5185: \\t// Optional: Path at which the If this\\n5219: \\t// is not set, reads from stdin in the container will always result in EOF.\\n5220: \\t// Default is false.\\n5221: \\t// +optional\\n5222: \\tStdin bool `json:\\\"stdin,omitempty\\\" protobuf:\\\"varint,16,opt,name=stdin\\\"`\\n5223: \\t// Whether the container runtime should close the stdin channel after it has been opened by\\n5224: \\t// a single attach. When stdin is true the stdin stream will remain open across multiple attach\\n5225: \\t// sessions. If stdinOnce is set to true, stdin is opened on container start, is empty until the\\n5226: \\t// first client attaches to stdin, and then remains open and accepts data until the client disconnects,\\n5227: \\t// at which time stdin is closed and remains closed until the container is restarted. All fields must be kept in sync between\\n5239: // these two types.\\n5240: var _ = Container(EphemeralContainerCommon{})\\n5241: \\n5242: // An EphemeralContainer is a temporary container that you may add to an existing Pod for\\n5243: // user-initiated activities such as debugging. Ephemeral containers have no resource or\\n5244: // scheduling guarantees, and they will not be restarted when they exit or when a Pod is\\n5245: // removed or restarted. The kubelet may evict a Pod if an ephemeral container causes the\\n5246: // Pod to exceed its resource allocation.\\n5247: //\\n5248: // To add an ephemeral container, use the ephemeralcontainers subresource of an existing\\n5249: // Pod. Ephemeral containers may not be removed or restarted.\\n5250: type EphemeralContainer struct {\\n5251: \\t// Ephemeral containers have all of the fields of Container, plus additional fields\\n5252: \\t// specific to ephemeral containers. Fields in common with Container are in the\\n5253: \\t// following inlined struct so than an EphemeralContainer may easily be converted\\n5254: \\t// to a Container.\\n5255: \\tEphemeralContainerCommon `json:\\\",inline\\\" protobuf:\\\"bytes,1,req\\\"`\\n5256: \\n5257: \\t// If set, the name of the container from PodSpec that this ephemeral container targets.\\n5258: \\t// The ephemeral container will be run in the namespaces (IPC, PID, etc) of this container.\\n5259: \\t// If not set then the ephemeral container uses the namespaces configured in the Pod spec.\\n5260: \\t//\\n5261: \\t// The container runtime must implement support for this feature. If the runtime does not\\n5262: \\t// support namespace targeting then the result of setting this field is undefined.\\n5263: \\t// +optional\\n5264: \\tTargetContainerName string `json:\\\"targetContainerName,omitempty\\\" protobuf:\\\"bytes,2,opt,name=targetContainerName\\\"`\\n5265: }\\n5266: \\n5267: // PodStatus represents information about the status of a pod. Status may trail the actual\\n5268: // state of a system, especially if the node that hosts the pod cannot contact the control\\n5269: // plane.\\n5270: type PodStatus struct {\\n5271: \\t// If set, this represents the .metadata.generation that the pod status was set based upon.\\n5272: \\t// The PodObservedGenerationTracking feature gate must be enabled to use this field.\\n5273: \\t// +optional\\n5274: \\tObservedGeneration int64 `json:\\\"observedGeneration,omitempty\\\" protobuf:\\\"varint,17,opt,name=observedGeneration\\\"`\\n5275: \\t// The phase of a Pod is a simple, high-level summary of where the Pod is in its lifecycle.\\n5276: \\t// The conditions array, the reason and message fields, and the individual container status\\n5277: \\t// arrays contain more detail about the pod's status.\\n5278: \\t// There are five possible phase values:\\n5279: \\t//\\n5280: \\t// Pending: The pod has been accepted by the Kubernetes system, but one or more of the\\n5281: \\t// container images has not been created. This includes time before being scheduled as\\n5282: \\t// well as time spent downloading images over the network, which could take a while.\\n5283: \\t// Running: The pod has been bound to a node, and all of the containers have been created.\\n5284: \\t// At least one container is still running, or is in the process of starting or restarting.\\n5285: \\t// Succeeded: All containers in the pod have terminated in success, and will not be restarted.\\n5286: \\t// Failed: All containers in the pod have terminated, and at least one container has\\n5287: \\t// terminated in failure. The container either exited with non-zero status or was terminated\\n5288: \\t// by the system.\\n5289: \\t// Unknown: For some reason the state of the pod could not be obtained, typically due to an\\n5290: \\t// error in communicating with the host of the pod.\\n5291: \\t//\\n5292: \\t// More info: https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/workloads/pods/pod-lifecycle#pod-phase\\n5293: \\t// +optional\\n5294: \\tPhase PodPhase `json:\\\"phase,omitempty\\\" protobuf:\\\"bytes,1,opt,name=phase,casttype=PodPhase\\\"`\\n5295: \\t// Current service state of pod.\\n5296: \\t// More info: https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/workloads/pods/pod-lifecycle#pod-conditions\\n5297: \\t// +optional\\n5298: \\t// +patchMergeKey=type\\n5299: \\t// +patchStrategy=merge\\n5300: \\t// +listType=map\\n5301: \\t// +listMapKey=type\\n5302: \\tConditions []PodCondition `json:\\\"conditions,omitempty\\\" patchStrategy:\\\"merge\\\" patchMergeKey:\\\"type\\\" protobuf:\\\"bytes,2,rep,name=conditions\\\"`\\n5303: \\t// A human readable message indicating details about why the pod is in this condition.\\n5304: \\t// +optional\\n5305: \\tMessage string `json:\\\"message,omitempty\\\" protobuf:\\\"bytes,3,opt,name=message\\\"`\\n5306: \\t// A brief CamelCase message indicating details about why the pod is in this state.\\n5307: \\t// e.g. 'Evicted'\\n5308: \\t// +optional\\n5309: \\tReason string `json:\\\"reason,omitempty\\\" protobuf:\\\"bytes,4,opt,name=reason\\\"`\\n5310: \\t// nominatedNodeName is set only when this pod preempts other pods on the node, but it cannot be\\n5311: \\t// scheduled right away as preemption victims receive their graceful termination periods.\\n5312: \\t// This field does not guarantee that the pod will be scheduled on this node. If this is not specified, the value\\n6224: \\t// of the 'port' field is used (an identity map).\\n6225: \\t// This field is ignored for services with clusterIP=None, and should be\\n6226: \\t// omitted or set equal to the 'port' field.\\n6227: \\t// More info: https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/services-networking/service/#defining-a-service\\n6228: \\t// +optional\\n6229: \\tTargetPort intstr.IntOrString `json:\\\"targetPort,omitempty\\\" protobuf:\\\"bytes,4,opt,name=targetPort\\\"`\\n6230: \\n6231: \\t// The port on each node on which this service is exposed when type is\\n6232: \\t// NodePort or LoadBalancer. in bytes.\\n6664: \\t// +optional\\n6665: \\tCapacity *int64 `json:\\\"capacity,omitempty\\\" protobuf:\\\"varint,1,opt,name=capacity\\\"`\\n6666: }\\n6667: \\n6668: // NodeConfigStatus describes the status of the config assigned by Node.Spec.ConfigSource.\\n6669: type NodeConfigStatus struct {\\n6670: \\t// Assigned reports the checkpointed config the node will try to use.\\n6671: \\t// When Node.Spec.ConfigSource is updated, the node checkpoints the associated\\n6672: \\t// config payload to local disk, along with a record indicating intended\\n6673: \\t// config. The node refers to this record to choose its config checkpoint, and\\n6674: \\t// reports this record in Assigned. Assigned only updates in the status after\\n6675: \\t// the record has been checkpointed to disk. When the Kubelet is restarted,\\n6676: \\t// it tries to make the Assigned config the Active config by loading and\\n6677: \\t// validating the checkpointed payload identified by Assigned.\\n6678: \\t// +optional\\n6679: \\tAssigned *NodeConfigSource `json:\\\"assigned,omitempty\\\" protobuf:\\\"bytes,1,opt,name=assigned\\\"`\\n6680: \\t// Active reports the checkpointed config the node is actively using.\\n6681: \\t// Active will represent either the current version of the Assigned config,\\n6682: \\t// or the current LastKnownGood config, depending on whether attempting to use the\\n6683: \\t// Assigned config results in an error.\\n6684: \\t// +optional\\n6685: \\tActive *NodeConfigSource `json:\\\"active,omitempty\\\" protobuf:\\\"bytes,2,opt,name=active\\\"`\\n6686: \\t// LastKnownGood reports the checkpointed config the node will fall back to\\n6687: \\t// when it encounters an error attempting to use the Assigned config.\\n6688: \\t// The Assigned config becomes the LastKnownGood config when the node determines\\n6689: \\t// that the Assigned config is stable and correct.\\n6690: \\t// This is currently implemented as a 10-minute soak period starting when the local\\n6691: \\t// record of Assigned config is updated. If the Assigned config is Active at the end\\n6692: \\t// of this period, it becomes the LastKnownGood. Note that if Spec.ConfigSource is\\n6693: \\t// reset to nil (use local defaults), the LastKnownGood is also immediately reset to nil,\\n6694: \\t// because the local default config is always assumed good.\\n6695: \\t// You should not make assumptions about the node's method of determining config stability\\n6696: \\t// and correctness, as this may change or become configurable in the future.\\n6697: \\t// +optional\\n6698: \\tLastKnownGood *NodeConfigSource `json:\\\"lastKnownGood,omitempty\\\" protobuf:\\\"bytes,3,opt,name=lastKnownGood\\\"`\\n6699: \\t// Error describes any problems reconciling the Spec.ConfigSource to the Active config.\\n6700: \\t// Errors may occur, for example, attempting to checkpoint Spec.ConfigSource to the local Assigned\\n6701: \\t// record, attempting to checkpoint the payload associated with Spec.ConfigSource, attempting\\n6702: \\t// to load or validate the Assigned config, etc.\\n6703: \\t// Errors may occur at different points while syncing config. Earlier errors (e.g. download or\\n6704: \\t// checkpointing errors) will not result in a rollback to LastKnownGood, and may resolve across\\n6705: \\t// Kubelet retries. Later errors (e.g. loading or validating a checkpointed config) will result in\\n6706: \\t// a rollback to LastKnownGood. In the latter case, it is usually possible to resolve the error\\n6707: \\t// by fixing the config assigned in Spec.ConfigSource.\\n6708: \\t// You can find additional information for debugging by searching the error message in the Kubelet log.\\n6709: \\t// Error is a human-readable description of the error state; machines can check whether or not Error\\n6710: \\t// is empty, but should not rely on the stability of the Error text across Kubelet versions.\\n6711: \\t// +optional\\n6712: \\tError string `json:\\\"error,omitempty\\\" protobuf:\\\"bytes,4,opt,name=error\\\"`\\n6713: = \\\"Terminated\\\"\\n6860: )\\n6861: \\n6862: type NodeConditionType string\\n6863: \\n6864: // These are valid but not exhaustive conditions of node. (\\n6867: \\t// NodeReady means kubelet is healthy and ready to accept pods.\\n6868: \\tNodeReady NodeConditionType = \\\"Ready\\\"\\n6869: \\t// NodeMemoryPressure means the kubelet is under pressure `json:\\\"message,omitempty\\\" protobuf:\\\"bytes,6,opt,name=message\\\"`\\n6897: }\\n6898: \\n6899: type NodeAddressType string\\n6900: \\n6901: // These are built-in addresses type of node. A cloud provider may set a type not listed here.\\n6902: const (\\n6903: \\t// NodeHostName identifies a name of the node. Although every node can be assumed\\n6904: \\t// to have a NodeAddress of this type, its exact syntax and semantics are not\\n6905: \\t// defined, and are not consistent between different clusters.\\n6906: \\tNodeHostName NodeAddressType = \\\"Hostname\\\"\\n6907: \\n6908: \\t// NodeInternalIP identifies an IP address which is assigned to one of the node's\\n6909: \\t// network interfaces. Every node should have at least one address of this type.\\n6910: \\t//\\n6911: \\t// An internal IP is normally expected to be reachable from every other node, but\\n6912: \\t// may not be visible to hosts outside the cluster. By default it is assumed that\\n6913: \\t// kube-apiserver can reach node internal IPs, though it is possible to configure\\n6914: \\t// clusters where this is not the case.\\n6915: \\t//\\n6916: \\t// NodeInternalIP is the default type of node IP, and does not necessarily imply\\n6917: \\t// that the IP is ONLY reachable internally. If a node has multiple internal IPs,\\n6918: \\t// no specific semantics are assigned to the additional IPs.\\n6919: \\tNodeInternalIP NodeAddressType = \\\"InternalIP\\\"\\n6920: \\n6921: \\t// NodeExternalIP identifies an IP address which is, in some way, intended to be\\n6922: \\t// more usable from outside the cluster then an internal IP, though no specific\\n6923: \\t// semantics are defined. It may be a globally routable IP, though it is not\\n6924: \\t// required to be.\\n6925: \\t//\\n6926: \\t// External IPs may be assigned directly to an interface on the node, like a\\n6927: \\t// NodeInternalIP, or alternatively, packets sent to the external IP may be NAT'ed\\n6928: \\t// to an internal node IP rather than being delivered directly (making the IP less\\n6929: \\t// efficient for node-to-node traffic than a NodeInternalIP).\\n6930: \\tNodeExternalIP NodeAddressType = \\\"ExternalIP\\\"\\n6931: \\n6932: \\t// NodeInternalDNS identifies a DNS name which resolves to an IP address which has\\n6933: \\t// the characteristics of a NodeInternalIP. The IP it resolves to may or may not\\n6934: \\t// be a listed NodeInternalIP address.\\n6935: \\tNodeInternalDNS NodeAddressType = \\\"InternalDNS\\\"\\n6936: \\n6937: \\t// NodeExternalDNS identifies a DNS name which resolves to an IP address which has\\n6938: \\t// the characteristics of a NodeExternalIP. // ---\\n7387: // New uses of this type are discouraged because of difficulty describing its usage when embedded in APIs.\\n7388: //  1. Ignored fields.  It includes many fields which are not generally honored.  For instance, ResourceVersion and FieldPath are both very rarely valid in actual usage.\\n7389: //  2. Invalid usage help.  It is impossible to add specific help for individual usage.  In most embedded usages, there are particular\\n7390: //     restrictions like, \\\"must refer only to types A and B\\\" or \\\"UID not honored\\\" or \\\"name must be restricted\\\".\\n7391: //     Those cannot be well described when embedded.\\n7392: //  3. Inconsistent validation.  Because the usages are different, the validation rules are different by usage, which makes it hard for users to predict what will happen.\\n7393: //  4. The fields are both imprecise and overly precise.  Kind is not a precise mapping to a URL. This can produce ambiguity\\n7394: //     during interpretation and require a REST mapping.  In most cases, the dependency is on the group,resource tuple\\n7395: //     and the version of the actual struct is irrelevant.\\n7396: //  5. We cannot easily change it.  Because this type is embedded in many locations, updates to this type\\n7397: //     will affect numerous schemas.  Don't make new APIs embed an underspecified API type they do not control.\\n7398: //\\n7399: // Instead of using this type, create a locally provided and used type that is well-focused on your reference.\\n7400: // For example, ServiceReferences for admission registration: https://github.com/kubernetes/api/blob/release-1.17/admissionregistration/v1/types.go#L533 .\\n7401: //
